title: Congratulations
wikitext:
[]: Congratulations?             Quote Post by ruffmanl » Fri Jul 19, 2013 12:42 am So this is going to take a while and a lot of explanation to set up this question, but bear with me. So when I first watched the ending of the television series, I interpretted the ending as meaning that Shinji accepted the Human Instrumentality Project (I hadn't seen the movie yet, so I didn't even know that he had a choice in the matter), and they were all congratulating him because he decided to live happily in his fake reality inside the primodial ooze filled with all of earth's spirits, rather than spending eternity creating a world filled with self loathing around himself. Now, after watching End of Evangelion, i came to believe that the entirety for Episodes 25 and Episodes 26 take place within the end of EoE. Enough has already been said and pointed out about all the similarities of the two that it has to be within the same cannon (the shot Katsuragi and the dead Ritsuko). But herein lies where I begin to not understand. Up until the Third Impact, I was positive that these were both within the same cannon. The beginning of EoE took place before the final two episodes, and it wasn't until the Third Impact and Instrumentality and the "tang-ing" of everybody until the final two episodes take place. But in the movie Shinji decides that he rather live in a reality filled with sadness than a fake world filled with empty happiness. So up until this point, I was under the impression that the two final episodes took place within the movie. Yet they seem to have two completely different endings. Now are the questions. 1. Do the movies truly take place within the same cannon? Do the two final episodes take place within the movie (the beginning of the movie were the events that lead up to Episodes 25 and 26)? Or are the events which cause the final two episodes in the series completely different than the events depicted in the movie? 2. If the movies are the same canon, at what point do they veer off into two separate endings? If the two endings are indeed two possibilties (the TV accepting instrumentality, the movie denouncing it), at what point in the movie do the events change from the events depicted in the television show? 3. Or am I completely wrong in assuming the congratulations are him getting praised for accepting instrumentality and really he is being congratulated for accepted reality over the facade he was given? I first tried to check out the wiki for answers, but the article on this (and arguably one of the most important questions of the series) is rather lacking. Entire sections of the wiki page are empty and the grammar and spelling leaves a lot to be desired. Here's the link to the article in question: http://wiki.evageeks.org/Theory_and_Analysis:EoTV_vs._EoE

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Fri Jul 19, 2013 1:44 am This is one of the major points of controversy in interpretation, how the very different endings could or should be reconciled. Personally I treat them as alternate game endings (like Gendo True End and Shinji Bad End respectively) As expected, there are a lot of related threads, including http://forum.evageeks.org/thread/13243/EoTV-EoE-Narrative-Concurrency-Reasons-for-and-Against/ http://forum.evageeks.org/thread/8122/An-EoE-Theory-thats-been-confusing-me-Concurrency/ http://forum.evageeks.org/thread/12277/Ep-25-Script-and-its-Implications-Concurrency/ http://forum.evageeks.org/thread/10494/Concurrency-Rises-from-the-Grave/ http://forum.evageeks.org/thread/9421/Does-Yamagas-statement-about-EoTVvsEoE-affect-concurrency/ http://forum.evageeks.org/thread/8141/What-isConcurrency/ http://forum.evageeks.org/thread/1833/EoTV-EoE-Concurrency-and-Gainax/ http://forum.evageeks.org/thread/14951/Your-Attitudes-to-Continuity-and-One-True-Canon/

[]: Re: Congratulations?             Quote Post by ObsessiveMathsFreak » Sun Jul 21, 2013 10:22 am ruffmanl wrote: So when I first watched the ending of the television series, I interpretted the ending as meaning that Shinji accepted the Human Instrumentality Project (I hadn't seen the movie yet, so I didn't even know that he had a choice in the matter), and they were all congratulating him because he decided to live happily in his fake reality inside the primodial ooze filled with all of earth's spirits, rather than spending eternity creating a world filled with self loathing around himself.

[]:             Quote Post by Caliburn » Mon Jul 22, 2013 12:53 am Just to add to the reading list, I'm sure it's referenced within some of the threads that Mr. Tines has cited, but I like MDWigs' argument that Shinji rejected Instrumentality in both EoTV and EoE: http://www.angelfire.com/anime4/mdwigs/endings.html This is essentially how I have always interpreted the endings as well: in both cases, Shinji overcomes his depression and decides that his life is worth living.

[]:             Quote Post by Kendrix » Thu Jul 25, 2013 4:54 pm The easiest explanation, of course, is that EoTV!Shinji happened to detang on his fifteenth birthday.

[]:             Quote Post by Atropos » Thu Jul 25, 2013 5:11 pm I understand both endings as the rejection of Instrumentality, but not under the same circumstances at all (yes, I favor the 'DC episodes are an alternate universe' theory). The most notable change seems to be that Yui chooses to return to human form at the end of EoTV (as she's shown next to Gendo with the rest of the cast at the end).

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Thu Jul 25, 2013 8:09 pm I don't think the final image of the episode is mean to be taken literally, or that it says who returned to human form or whatever. If you insist on putting it in narrative terms, even though traditional narrative has basically been discarded at that point, Shinji clearly remains trapped in Instrumentality at the end of the TV series, having shaped it into a masturbatory solipsistic little world where everyone praises him. EoTV can't really be understood without reading End, in which it becomes clear that EoTV is a period of manic energy that leads to a false epiphany and EoE is the crash from that manic stage and the deepening of depression at the realization how much work it'll take to get out of it. Shinji has failed to complete the process of ego destruction and cross the abyss, and fallen down the left hand path instead, becoming trapped within himself.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Fri Jul 26, 2013 1:30 am Chuckman wrote: Shinji clearly remains trapped in Instrumentality at the end of the TV series, having shaped it into a masturbatory solipsistic little world where everyone praises him.

[]:             Quote Post by Kendrix » Fri Jul 26, 2013 6:48 pm Atropos wrote: The most notable change seems to be that Yui chooses to return to human form at the end of EoTV .

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Fri Jul 26, 2013 7:12 pm I dunno, man, I just think the main character and author insert standing on a big blue ball while everyone figuratively pats him on the back and congratulating him for his epiphany rings hollow. You don't just decide you're not depressed anymore. There's a substantive difference between having the big epiphany and getting that burst of energy and following it through to make actual change when that energy runs out, and that's what the show/movie dichotomy is talking about, on that particular level of its meaning. EoTV is just not as mature as EoE, whose ending is gritty and kind of a downer but the main characters are alive and ready to keep struggling. Yui may be a crazy bitch but the key message of her little speech -that it's better to be alive than not- is an important one and it's part of the puzzle that the TV ending is missing. Hence my feeling that EoE supersedes EoTV. It covers the same ground, but it's a second draft, and the message has more meaning but it depends on EoTV to create that meaning. EoTV is so trippy in its imagery I don't think it's meant to represent something that physically happened, like the ending of the show is the main cast reappearing somewhere clapping for Shinji. It's a metaphor. Obviously my reading that he accepts Instrumentality or some kind of non physical state that amounts to a fantasy world differs from the author's intent, but that's what I see on the screen and that's what it looks like to me. If EoE simply didn't exist I might interpret it different, but they need to be reconciled- not on a canonical or mere simulation level but on a thematic one, there has to be a reason why they're so different a "spite at the audience" or more money for animation or whatever is a glib, superficial answer that betrays the artistic merit of the works.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Sat Jul 27, 2013 2:00 am Chuckman wrote: EoTV is so trippy in its imagery I don't think it's meant to represent something that physically happened, like the ending of the show is the main cast reappearing somewhere clapping for Shinji. It's a metaphor. Obviously my reading that he accepts Instrumentality or some kind of non physical state that amounts to a fantasy world differs from the author's intent
